title: CVE, CT & Stabilization – Somalia
starter_prompts: What are the CVE entry points in al-Shabaab-controlled or -influenced areas?
How do I engage communities in recently liberated territories?
What are the risks of being associated with military actors in stabilization work?
How does al-Shabaab's taxation system affect our operations?
What does a defector reintegration pathway look like in practice?

sources.title: Africa Center for Strategic Studies – Practical Lessons to Improve Stabilization Operations in Somalia
Africa Center for Strategic Studies – AMISOM's Hard-Earned Lessons in Somalia
Clingendael / NOREF – Red Lines and al-Shabaab: Negotiating Humanitarian Access in Somalia
ODI – A Deadly Dilemma: How Al-Shabaab Came to Dictate the Terms of Humanitarian Aid in Somalia
GSDRC – Talking to the Other Side: Humanitarian Negotiations with Al-Shabaab in Somalia
OsloMet – Countering Violent Extremism in Somalia and Kenya
Georgetown Journal of International Affairs – Countering Violent Extremism in the Horn of Africa
Vanda Felbab-Brown / UNU – Reintegration in Somalia
Combating Terrorism Center at West Point – Somalia's Stalled Offensive Against al-Shabaab
Combating Terrorism Center at West Point – Somalia at a Crossroads: Resurgent Insurgents and the Future of AUSSOM
The New Humanitarian – Al-Shabaab's Shadow State: Why Somalia's Militants Are Winning Legitimacy
UN Somalia (UNSOM) – Prevention and Countering of Violent Extremism
International Peace Institute – The UN Support Office Model: Lessons from Somalia (2024)

situation_overview: Al-Shabaab is a designated terrorist organization that controls significant territory across South Central Somalia and operates as a shadow government — collecting taxes, adjudicating disputes, delivering services, and enforcing compliance — often more effectively than the Federal Government of Somalia (FGS). The FGS launched a major offensive in August 2022 alongside clan militias (Ma'awisley), recapturing over 215 locations in Hirshabelle and Galmudug by April 2023. However, the offensive stalled through 2024 and reached a stalemate by early 2025. By mid-2025, al-Shabaab had recaptured most of the territory it lost in Middle Shabelle since 2022, including the strategic town of Adan Yabaal (April 2025). The 'hold' and 'build' phases have consistently failed due to poor civil-military coordination, SNA command-and-control fragmentation, and FGS governance gaps. A parallel and distinct threat — Islamic State-Somalia — operates in Puntland, where it serves as a global financing hub for the Islamic State network; Puntland is leading CT operations against IS-Somalia largely without FGS or AU support. Political fragmentation is the central obstacle to CT progress: Puntland has boycotted the National Consultative Council since January 2023; Jubaland-FGS tensions led to armed clashes in late 2024; and constitutional disputes have diverted presidential attention from the al-Shabaab fight. The African Union Support and Stabilization Mission in Somalia (AUSSOM), which replaced ATMIS in January 2025, faces a severe funding crisis — less than 20% of its Jan-June 2025 costs were covered as of publication. Field operators work in a complex environment where al-Shabaab controls access, taxes humanitarian operations, and leverages counter-terrorism financing laws to suppress engagement. CVE and stabilization work must be differentiated by territorial control zone: al-Shabaab permanent control, al-Shabaab semi-permanent presence, contested, and FGS-controlled.

armed_groups.name: Al-Shabaab (Harakat al-Shabaab al-Mujahideen)
armed_groups.geography: Dominant in South Central Somalia (Jubaland, South West State, Bay, Bakool, Hiraan). Active in all regions. Periodic attacks in Mogadishu and Puntland. In South West State specifically: controls four full districts (one-third of territory) and influences rural areas and main roads from Mogadishu to Baidoa to Luuq and from Mogadishu to Afgoye to Baraawe.
armed_groups.status: Designated terrorist organization by US, UN, EU, AU. Controls or contests approximately 20–30% of Somali territory. Has resisted SNA/AUSSOM offensive and reconstituted in cleared areas. As of mid-2025, has recaptured most of the territory lost in Middle Shabelle since 2022, including Adan Yabaal. Conducting attacks in Lower Shabelle and in/around Mogadishu. Deliberately waited until after ATMIS drawdown was complete before launching its 2025 counteroffensive.
armed_groups.engagement_notes: Al-Shabaab operates a Humanitarian Coordination Office (HCO) in areas under its control. The HCO monitors, regulates, registers, and taxes NGOs. Organizations that maintain structured dialogue at all levels — from senior leadership shura to local commanders — have the best track record of sustained access. Single-level engagement is insufficient. Counter-terrorism financing laws (US, EU) create serious legal risk for direct financial transactions; legal counsel should be engaged before operations in AS-controlled areas. Al-Shabaab's propaganda is increasingly effective at narrative shaping — attacks near Mogadishu serve as much to undermine government legitimacy as to achieve tactical objectives.
armed_groups.risks: Al-Shabaab has expelled numerous NGOs, including major UN agencies during the 2011 famine. Expulsion is typically triggered by: perceived government/military affiliation, public criticism, failure to pay 'registration fees', or operating without HCO clearance. Staff detention and targeted killings of perceived informants have occurred. Do not underestimate al-Shabaab's intelligence capacity — they often know staff identities and affiliations. Reversals of government gains send a signal to communities everywhere about the permanence of FGS security provision — al-Shabaab leverages these reversals actively.

armed_groups.name: Somali National Army (SNA)
armed_groups.geography: Nationwide, primary operations in South Central Somalia
armed_groups.status: Primary CT/stabilization partner. Significant capacity gaps in logistics, intelligence, and hold-phase operations. Clan composition influences unit loyalty and operational behavior by region. The SNA force generation model is structurally fragmented: troops are recruited, trained, and equipped through separate bilateral programs (US, Turkey, UAE, EU, Eritrea, Uganda), resulting in loosely integrated forces with limited central command. Unit commanders are frequently reshuffled, disrupting stability, preparations, and training.
armed_groups.engagement_notes: SNA association creates neutrality risk for humanitarian and CVE actors. Clan affiliation of SNA units varies by region and affects community perception of operations. Do not assume SNA units in a given area share command structure, operational doctrine, or even chain of command — the 'dysfunctional command and control' noted by analysts means unit behavior is highly variable. Ma'awisley (clan militia) integration alongside SNA was the most consequential factor in early 2022 gains; SNA units without equivalent clan buy-in perform significantly worse.
armed_groups.risks: Being seen as embedded with or funded by SNA operations will compromise access in al-Shabaab areas and reduce community trust in CVE programs. Civil-military coordination is essential but must be structured to maintain visible separation. SNA forces with minimal training have been placed in security responsibility roles previously held by AU forces — do not assume professional military standards in post-clearance areas.

armed_groups.name: AUSSOM / AMISOM (African Union Transition Mission in Somalia)
armed_groups.geography: Major bases: Mogadishu, Kismaayo, Baidoa, Beledweyne. Sector-based deployment across four sectors.
armed_groups.status: AUSSOM replaced ATMIS on January 1, 2025. Authorized at up to 11,911 personnel (Uganda 4,500, Ethiopia 2,500, Djibouti 1,520, Kenya 1,410, Egypt 1,091). Burundi opted out. As of April 2025, TCCs stressed the need for at least 8,000 additional personnel. Facing a severe funding crisis: less than 20% of Jan-June 2025 costs were mobilized. ATMIS handed over 21 FOBs to Somali security forces and withdrew 9,000 troops across three drawdown phases — these positions were not fully replaced by SNA forces as planned, creating gaps al-Shabaab exploited.
armed_groups.engagement_notes: AUSSOM has progressively adopted COIN tactics but civil-military coordination with humanitarian actors remains weak. Coordinate through UN CMCoord mechanisms; avoid direct operational embedding. Egypt's entry as a new TCC reflects geopolitical realignment (Egypt-Somalia defense pact, August 2024) driven partly by Ethiopia-Egypt tensions, not Somalia stabilization priorities — factor this into assessments of AUSSOM cohesion.
armed_groups.risks: AUSSOM funding crisis is creating operational uncertainty. If the mission fails to secure sustainable financing, security vacuums will expand rapidly. The drawdown-without-replacement pattern from ATMIS is already documented — plan operations around this risk. Do not assume AUSSOM coverage in any area is stable; reassess regularly.

armed_groups.name: Islamic State-Somalia (ISS)
armed_groups.type: Jihadist group / global Islamic State affiliate
armed_groups.geography: Primary: Bari region of Puntland (Al-Miskaad mountains). Minimal significant presence in South Central Somalia. Not a meaningful operational factor outside Puntland.
armed_groups.status: Designated terrorist organization. Significantly smaller than al-Shabaab (estimated 600-700 fighters, some estimates up to 1,600). Despite small domestic footprint, IS-Somalia's al-Karrar coordination office reportedly oversees financing for IS affiliates worldwide — confirmed by AFRICOM General Langley in 2025 Posture Statement. Has attracted foreign fighters from Yemen, Tanzania, Libya, Algeria, Tunisia, Sudan, Ethiopia, Syria. Has begun deploying drone attacks against Puntland security forces (January 2025).
armed_groups.engagement_notes: Puntland is leading ground operations against IS-Somalia through Operation Hilaac (launched January 2025) with approximately 3,000 forces. US and UAE are providing airstrikes in coordination. The FGS provides minimal support to Puntland's IS-Somalia fight. Do not conflate IS-Somalia with al-Shabaab — they are rival organizations with different geographies, command structures, and engagement profiles.
armed_groups.risks: The dual-threat environment (al-Shabaab in the south, IS-Somalia in Puntland) creates resource competition. If Puntland deploys forces south to support FGS against al-Shabaab (as signaled in April 2025), it weakens Operation Hilaac. IS-Somalia's global financing role means it attracts US/UAE CT attention disproportionate to its domestic Somalia profile — this external pressure can create unpredictable operational environments in Bari region.

armed_groups.name: Clan Militias / Local Armed Groups (including Ma'awisley)
armed_groups.geography: Distributed nationwide — Hawiye, Darod, Dir, Rahanweyn, and subclan variants. Ma'awisley most active in Hiiraan and Middle Shabelle.
armed_groups.status: Variable alignment — some allied with FGS, some with al-Shabaab, some independent. Critical to both al-Shabaab recruitment/support and community CVE efforts. Ma'awisley clan militia self-mobilization — particularly the Haber Gedir/Saleeban sub-clan of Hawiye — was the most consequential factor in the early 2022 SNA gains in Hiiraan and Middle Shabelle. This momentum was not replicated in Galmudug because the offensive expanded too quickly without first securing clan engagement.
armed_groups.engagement_notes: Clan structures are the primary social organizing unit in Somalia. No CVE, reintegration, or stabilization program succeeds without clan elder buy-in. Identify the relevant clan structure before any community engagement. Elder networks can provide early warning and access assurance that formal security actors cannot. The Ma'awisley model (clan militia fighting alongside national forces against al-Shabaab) demonstrates that clan mobilization can be a decisive CVE tool — but it requires sustained political and material support to hold.
armed_groups.risks: Engaging one clan's militia or elder network without equivalent engagement with others in contested areas can be perceived as partisan and create security risk. Clan militia support for the SNA is not permanent — Hawadle clan militias in Hiraan were reportedly fighting al-Shabaab on their own in May 2025 due to decline in federal military support, demonstrating that this alliance requires ongoing maintenance.

lessons: CVE interventions must be geographically differentiated: programs appropriate for FGS-controlled areas are counterproductive in al-Shabaab permanent-control areas. Map territorial control before designing any CVE activity.

Target vulnerable micro-groups, not whole communities. Drivers of radicalization — unemployment, clan grievance, ideology, coercion, family ties — vary significantly. Catchall community programs are ineffective and can signal weakness.

Government legitimacy is the central CVE variable. People comply with al-Shabaab primarily due to distrust of the FGS, not ideology. CVE programs that do not address governance gaps treat symptoms, not causes. Al-Shabaab's comparative advantage is service delivery: courts, taxation, dispute resolution, and enforcement. CVE interventions that cannot offer credible alternatives to these services have limited impact.

The reversal of government military gains sends a powerful counter-CVE signal to communities — it demonstrates that FGS security provision is impermanent. Stabilization programs must anticipate this and build community resilience that survives territorial fluctuations.

Somalia's National Strategy on P/CVE (2016) identifies seven pillars: dialogue and conflict prevention; governance and rule of law; community engagement; youth empowerment; gender equality; education and employment; and strategic communications. Use this as a coordination framework with government counterparts.

Women are critical but underutilized CVE actors. Female community members have insight into family-level radicalization pathways and early warning signals that male-dominated programming misses entirely.

The 'clear' phase has repeatedly succeeded; the 'hold' and 'build' phases have consistently failed. The core gap is civil-military coordination between SNA/AUSSOM and civilian stabilization actors in the post-clearance window. Resource constraints matter, but the lack of pre- and post-operation coordination between military and civilian authorities is an equal or greater constraint.

The 0–90 day window after military clearance is the most critical stabilization window. Somalia's National Stabilization Strategy calls for 'early recovery' activities (food, basic services, initial reconciliation dialogues) in this period. In practice, few civilian activities occur in this window because civilian agencies have not been pre-briefed or pre-positioned.

The military has a legal obligation under International Humanitarian Law (4th Geneva Convention) to maintain responsibility for civilian populations in a cleared area until civil authority is formally transferred. This transfer should happen as quickly as possible — military forces lack competence in governance functions and will rapidly lose legitimacy if seen as long-term administrators. There is also a risk of 'militarizing the humanitarian space' with negative implications for civilian agencies.

Al-Shabaab consistently reconstitutes in areas where the hold phase is weak. Field operators should treat any 'cleared' area as contested until civilian governance is demonstrably functional. South West State demonstrates the pattern: despite multiple military operations, four full districts remain under AS control because the hold phase has never been adequately resourced or coordinated.

Pre-operation coordination between military and civilian authorities — agreeing on sequencing, governance handover, and community engagement plans before clearance operations — is the single most important structural improvement available. Civilian agencies need significantly longer lead time than military operations allow; they must be brought into planning early despite operational security constraints.

'Deployable capabilities' must be pre-built: MOILGR and MOIS have identified the need for rapid-deployment packages of police, administrators, and judges to provide temporary governance and rule of law immediately after clearance. Without pre-positioned civilian capacity, cleared areas remain ungoverned and are quickly reclaimed.

Coordination in Somalia is structurally inadequate: state stabilization meetings occur quarterly at the ministerial level. Technical or working-level teams typically do not meet, share information, or plan together. The gap between federal/strategic level and state/tactical level is absolute. Increasing coordination meeting frequency from quarterly to monthly, and adding working-level technical meetings, is a minimum fix.

Decision-making and information are highly centralized — interagency contact is restricted to heads of organizations. Field operators cannot assume that senior-level agreements translate to working-level action. Build explicit information-sharing mechanisms at the technical level.

Military and civilian actors have different definitions of 'secure.' The military considers an area cleared when it can operate there. Civil administration requires security stable enough for civilian life, commerce, and service provision — a higher threshold. Build this explicitly into post-clearance handover criteria.

Clan mobilization (Ma'awisley) was the most consequential factor in early 2022 SNA gains in Hiiraan and Middle Shabelle. The offensive expanded to Galmudug without first securing equivalent clan militia engagement — the momentum was not replicated and the operational results were significantly worse. Clan engagement must precede or accompany military operations, not follow them.

Al-Shabaab deliberately waited until after ATMIS drawdown was complete before launching its 2025 counteroffensive in Middle Shabelle. This is a documented pattern: al-Shabaab times major operations to exploit transition gaps. Factor any AUSSOM/SNA transition into security planning with highest risk alerts.

Interclan reconciliation dialogues in stabilized areas have shown measurable impact when conducted before civil infrastructure repair, not after. Sequencing matters: political reconciliation first, physical reconstruction second.

The SNA's clan composition in a given operational area directly affects the community's perception of post-clearance security. Mismatched clan composition of holding forces is a known driver of al-Shabaab return.

Organizations that succeeded in maintaining access in al-Shabaab areas engaged comprehensively at all levels — senior shura leadership, mid-level commanders, and local fighters. Engagement at only one level is insufficient and often counterproductive.

Al-Shabaab's Humanitarian Coordination Office (HCO) must be engaged as a formal access negotiation counterpart in areas under AS control. Attempting to operate without HCO registration greatly increases expulsion risk.

Counter-terrorism financing laws (US, EU, UN) technically prohibit transactions with al-Shabaab, including 'registration fees' or 'taxation.' This creates an irresolvable legal dilemma for many international NGOs. Resolve your organization's legal position — in writing, with legal counsel — before operations. Do not leave field staff to navigate this without guidance.

The culture of silence around AS engagement (driven by CT law risk) has historically reduced collective learning. Where possible, coordinate discreetly with peer agencies on access approaches to avoid reinventing access agreements.

Al-Shabaab has expelled organizations that publicly criticized them, reported on abuses, or were perceived as information-gathering for military actors. Maintain strict operational security on access negotiations.

Principled humanitarian engagement (Humanity, Neutrality, Impartiality, Independence) is a recognized framework that al-Shabaab's HCO has shown some respect for — invoking these principles explicitly in access negotiations has documented value.

The FGS Serendi rehabilitation program provides the primary formal pathway: low-risk defectors go to regional transition centers for counseling, skills training, and micro-enterprise grants (~$1,500). Know this pathway and be able to refer to it.

Community acceptance is the single biggest bottleneck for successful reintegration. Ex-combatants who return to communities without prior reconciliation work face rejection, which significantly increases re-recruitment risk.
Clan elder involvement in reintegration is not optional — it is the mechanism. Reintegration decisions are effectively made at the clan level. External programs that bypass elders have poor outcomes.

Gender gaps are significant: the FGS DDR program has focused almost entirely on male defectors. Women who leave al-Shabaab face compounded stigma (combatant and 'dishonored') and require separate, gender-sensitive pathways.
The gap between ex-combatant expectations and community member perceptions is a consistent failure point. Both sides require preparation before reintegration — not just the returnee.

Severe unemployment, unstable security, and financial difficulty are the primary drivers of re-recruitment. Micro-enterprise grants address only one of these. Programs without a security stabilization component in the operating area have high recidivism rates.
Al-Shabaab has sophisticated intelligence capacity — do not assume operational anonymity. Staff identities, organizational affiliations, and funding sources are often known before field teams arrive.

Association with US, EU, or other CT-funding sources is a known access risk in AS-controlled areas. Funding transparency requirements conflict directly with operational security needs — address this in your organizational security policy before deployment.
Establish a clear organizational policy on 'taxation' by armed groups before deployment. Field staff must know the answer to 'what do we do if AS demands payment' before it happens, not during.
Mogadishu-based security assumptions do not transfer to South Central Somalia field operations. Conduct zone-specific threat assessments for each operating area.

The AUSSOM transition and funding crisis is creating new and expanding security vacuums. Areas previously covered by ATMIS forces were handed to SNA with incomplete force generation — these areas are highest risk for rapid deterioration. Reassess access and movement protocols in post-ATMIS transitioning areas continuously.

Do not conflate al-Shabaab (South Central Somalia) and Islamic State-Somalia (Puntland/Bari region) in threat assessments or engagement protocols. They are rival organizations with different geographies and command structures.

Political infighting between FGS and Federal Member States is the primary driver of stalled CT and stabilization operations. The NCC (National Consultative Council) has failed to function as a genuine consensus-building mechanism. Puntland has boycotted NCC since January 2023; Jubaland-FGS relations deteriorated into armed clashes in late 2024.

Federal-FMS political tensions create direct security risks for field operators: the Jubaland-FGS armed confrontation at Ras Kamboni (late 2024) demonstrated that intra-Somali political disputes can turn kinetic, and al-Shabaab consistently exploits these windows to attack both sides.

The FGS constitutional amendment process (approved March 2024) — which expanded presidential powers, abolished the prime minister position, and introduced universal suffrage — has deepened political divisions and diverted presidential attention from CT operations. Track this political track as a key variable in stabilization assessments.

Clan consensus is as important as formal political agreement. President Mohamud has faced difficulty rallying support from his own Abgaal sub-clan during the 2025 al-Shabaab counteroffensive. No CT or stabilization initiative that lacks clan buy-in at multiple levels will hold.

The 4.5 power-sharing formula (distributing political representation among Hawiye, Darood, Digil-Mirifle, Dir, and minority coalition) structures all Somali political decision-making. Factor this into any engagement strategy with government counterparts — the clan identity of your interlocutor shapes their political incentives.

FMS (Federal Member States) have independent security and political relationships with al-Shabaab. Federal government coordination does not automatically transfer to member state operations. Always identify whether you are operating under FGS or FMS authority and map the relevant political dynamics separately.

lessons.category: AUSSOM, External Actors, and Mission Transition
lessons: AUSSOM faces a structural funding crisis: less than 20% of its January-June 2025 costs were mobilized as of publication. The US is blocking application of UNSCR 2719 assessed contributions. The EU has been cutting annual contributions. TCCs are owed outstanding liabilities of approximately $93.9 million from predecessor missions. This is not a temporary gap — plan operations around the risk of significant AUSSOM capability degradation.

The pattern from ATMIS-to-AUSSOM transition is clear: AU drawdowns created security gaps that were not replaced by Somali forces as planned, and al-Shabaab exploited these gaps to regain territory. This pattern will repeat unless force generation and capability transfer are specifically addressed.

External actors engage Somalia bilaterally in ways that fragment command and undermine coordination: SNA forces are trained and equipped separately by US, Turkey, UAE, EU, Eritrea, and Uganda, producing loosely integrated units with limited central command. This fragmentation is structural, not incidental.

Egypt's entry into Somalia's security architecture (defense pact August 2024, arms deliveries, AUSSOM participation) reflects Egypt-Ethiopia geopolitical tensions, not Somalia stabilization priorities. Egypt's motivations are partly about countering Ethiopia's regional influence. Factor this into assessments of external actor unity and reliability.

The Ethiopia-Somaliland MoU (January 2024) — which created a near-year-long diplomatic crisis between FGS and Addis Ababa — is a case study in how regional geopolitics can derail CT momentum. The diplomatic crisis drew FGS attention away from the al-Shabaab fight, complicated AUSSOM troop contributions, and created uncertainty over Ethiopian force presence in Somalia.

Turkey and the United States have provided consistent drone and airstrike support to FGS operations against al-Shabaab. The US under the current administration has increased airstrike tempo (16 strikes in the first three months of 2025) and loosened restrictions. But airstrikes alone cannot substitute for effective hold-phase operations — this is a documented lesson from every phase of the conflict.

The UN Integrated Task Force (ITF) coordinates UN political, security, and humanitarian actors in Somalia. Participate in ITF and CMCoord mechanisms to avoid duplicating access negotiations and to benefit from collective learning. Track OCHA Somalia's quarterly Humanitarian Access Overview — it provides the most current public assessment of access constraints by zone.

lessons.category: Engagement with Government and Coordination Architecture
lessons: Federal Member States (Jubaland, South West State, Hirshabelle, Galmudug, SSC-Khaatumo) have independent security and political relationships with al-Shabaab. Federal government coordination does not automatically transfer to member state operations.

NISA (National Intelligence and Security Agency) is the primary CT coordination counterpart for information sharing. NISA relationships are valuable but carry the same neutrality risk as SNA association.

The UN Integrated Task Force (ITF) coordinates UN political, security, and humanitarian actors in Somalia. Participate in ITF and CMCoord mechanisms to avoid duplicating access negotiations and to benefit from collective learning.
Track OCHA Somalia's quarterly Humanitarian Access Overview — it provides the most current public assessment of access constraints by zone and is the reference document for access planning.

Donors and external partners widely agree that the conflict with al-Shabaab cannot be resolved through military means alone. Political agreement within Somalia between FGS, FMS, and key stakeholders is a necessary condition for lasting stabilization gains. This is the single most important variable to track when assessing whether conditions for effective programming exist.
